text: The surgeon applies a clamp to the renal artery, selectively occluding the vessel supplying the upper pole of the kidney to prevent blood flow. The small tumor at the upper pole is carefully dissected and peeled away, with minimal bleeding observed due to the occlusion of the feeding vessel.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis by suturing the base of the kidney using a barbed V-lock suture with a large needle. The clamp on the renal artery is removed, restoring blood flow to the kidney, with minimal bleeding observed due to the tumor's location at the kidney's edge. The suture is run up the length of the base, effectively holding the tissue without slippage.

The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to snug down the barbed suture, running it along the outside of the kidney to close the defect. The barbs on the stitch help hold the kidney tissue together, allowing for precise tension adjustment with the clip.
